title: Sinus pressure and headache
summary: Facial pressure during a cold usually comes from inflamed nasal and sinus lining, not a bacterial infection. Pain relief, saline and short-term congestion treatment can help while monitoring for eye or neurological warning signs.
evidenceSnapshot: Analgesics have the clearest evidence for pain. Saline and short-term decongestants may help congestion; antibiotics are not routinely useful for early viral symptoms.

interventions.name: Paracetamol or ibuprofen
interventions.practical: Follow label and individual safety limits.

interventions.name: Saline spray or rinse
interventions.finding: May loosen mucus and improve drainage and comfort.
interventions.practical: Use safe water for irrigation and clean the device.
interventions.cautions: Stop if it causes persistent ear pain or nosebleeding.

interventions.name: Short-term decongestant
interventions.finding: Can reduce pressure when swelling is blocking drainage.
interventions.practical: Use a topical spray briefly or discuss pseudoephedrine with a pharmacist.

interventions.name: Warm compress
interventions.finding: May feel soothing over the cheeks or forehead but has not been shown to cure sinusitis.
interventions.practical: Use comfortably warm, not hot, for 10–15 minutes.
interventions.mechanism: May relax facial muscles and provide sensory comfort.
interventions.cautions: Avoid burns and do not delay care for eye symptoms.

practicalSteps: Check whether pain worsens when bending forward and whether smell is reduced.
Use saline first, then any prescribed nasal spray using correct technique.
Allow 7–10 days for an ordinary viral course while watching the direction of change.
avoidOrCare: Severe unilateral pain, dental symptoms or repeated episodes may need examination.
Steroid nasal sprays help allergy/chronic inflammation more than an ordinary cold and are not an instant decongestant.

myths.reality: Most early sinus symptoms are viral and resolve without antibiotics.
Colour reflects inflammation and immune cells more than the specific cause.

redFlags: Swollen, red or painful eye; vision change
Severe headache, neck stiffness, confusion or neurological change
Symptoms lasting more than 10 days without improvement, or 'double worsening' after initial improvement
unsettled: The exact point at which symptom duration and pattern best predict bacterial sinusitis varies between guidelines.
